Paragraph.Text: Two people sit across from each other. The voices and movements of the city streets can be heard outside, but neither person can hear it over the sound of their own silence. ROWAN, a short sixteen year old boy with a grungy style sits on a fainting couch staring off into space.
MARIANNE, Rowan's therapist, an older, professionally styled woman with sympathy in her eyes. Rowan hums out a reply.
Do you want to talk about it?
Would you like to start at the beginning? I find running through my own situations really helps me process them.
The therapist maintains eye contact and smiles, despite Rowan's avoidance.
I miss your mom too. Always such a kind soul.
The towing company returned her stuff from the crash yesterday.
Rowan reaches up and holds his necklace, a small silver cross.
Rowan relaxes at Marianne's comment, his movements slowing.
Are you prepared for the funeral tomorrow?

Rowan stands in front of the mirror, a look of exhaustion on his face as he takes in the black and white suit on his frame. Standing in his bathroom, brightly lit and a bit dirty, clearly missing a feminine touch. He struggles with his tie, not able to make the knot. Rowan's DAD walks by, an also short, stern-looking older man.
Let me give you a hand.
Rowan rolls his eyes, visibly annoyed, not looking at him. He drops the tie, surrendering it to his dad. Stepping in front of Rowan, his dad quickly begins tying the fabric.
Your mother was always the one to do this for me, but I guess I've learned a thing or two from her.
The tie comes together easily, matching his fathers. The two stand side by side facing the mirror, taking in the moment.
When I met Julie, it was New Years of 1999. We were-
At a party, I know. You've both told me a million times.
Give me a second. What we didn't tell you before is that we were both drunk. I was sprinting towards the bathroom when your mother ran into me and spilled red punch all over my white shirt.
Rowan watches his father become more animated than he has in weeks, the excitement of the story bleeding into him. He hangs onto his father's each word, laughing at his honesty.

She was so quick to apologize, two party hats lopsided on her head and the sparkliest dress I've ever seen flying towards me to push me into the bathroom. For what seemed like forever, we tried to scrub that stain out of my shirt, only making it worse. After that night we were inseparable.
Rowan and his father laugh at the retold memory, taking comfort in each other. Soon their faces relax, settling into a bittersweet smile. Tears well in Rowan's eyes.
Do you remember when mom said that I'm growing up too fast? That soon I won't need her anymore?
His dad says nothing, looking down at the floor.
I'm not sure that's true anymore.
Rowan begins crying. The room is silent besides Rowan's quiet sniffles. His dad grips his shoulders, forcing him to look up.
Alright, enough of that. Save your tears for the funeral. Is your speech ready?
Rowans shoulders fall. His dad stares at him expectedly. Rowan wipes his tears and reaches for the note in his pocket.
Yeah. Hey, is it alright if I take my own car? I want to rehearse one more time.
Sure, Rowan. Please don't be late.
Rowan's dad pats his sons shoulder, glancing one more time at his own reflection before leaving the bathroom. Rowan stands alone.
INT. ROWAN'S HOUSE - MOM'S STUDY - DAY
Standing in the doorway, Rowan moves towards the desk. Opening up his crumpled paper, he looks toward the chair she once sat at. Taking a deep, shaky breath, he begins to mumble out the speech.

As soon as he begins, the sound of his phone ringing interrupts him. RING. He sighs and fumbles for it in his pocket. He pulls it out and looks at the caller ID, sighing. It's SETH, Rowan's friend, with a laid-back tone of voice.
Waddup man, how you holding up?
Type shit, type shit. I heard the funeral's today. Wanted to check in.
Yeah, sorry I didn't invite you. Close family only.
Rowan pulls the phone away from his ear, wincing.
I thought I was family, Ro.
Rowan lets out a reluctant laugh.
No, but seriously. I'm really sorry about your mom.
Yeah, thanks. That means a lot. How have you been?
Got fired from the video store last week. Remember Martha, my boss? Absolute bitch.
Supposedly, they got me on camera stealing, but you know how it is. She's a liar.
Damn, I thought she was kinda nice.
Anyways, man. We haven't hung out in a while. It's been a minute. I was wondering if you wanted to pull up later, a few friends are coming over... We miss you...
The reality of the situation comes crashing back to Rowan. He glances down at the piece of paper in his hand, folding it back it up.
I'll let you know. Thanks for calling, Seth.

Before Seth has a chance to say goodbye, Rowan ends the call. The study is quiet again. Glancing at the clock on the wall, he realizes he's already late to the funeral. He makes eye contact with a photo of his mom.

Tossing the script to the side, he slumps to his mother's chair and falls into the seat. Rowan's made his decision. Tears slip down his face as he looks at the trinkets on her desk. Opening the desk drawers, he clutches old receipts, coupons, and mail to his chest. Sobbing now, he struggles to pick up a photo.
A candid picture of his mom, two lopsided party hats and the sparkliest dress he's ever seen adorning her.
The room is exactly how she left it. Despite being surrounded by pieces of his mom, the weight of her absence overwhelms him. Rowan's head falls into his hands.
The front door slams as Rowan's dad calls out his name.
Sighing, Rowan's dad loosens his tie as he stomps up the stairway. He rounds the corner to check Rowan's bedroom, seeing nothing but darkness.

He rushes down the hallway back towards the stairs. Stopping, he notices the cracked door to Julie's study. Slowly walking over and looking in, he opens the door. His face relaxes as he sees a dead asleep Rowan sprawled across his mom's desk. A surprised moment passes.
INT. ROWAN'S HOUSE - MOM'S STUDY - NIGHT
He gently shakes Rowan by the shoulder, and Rowan stirs awake, disoriented.
You finish that speech? You slept through the funeral.
Oh, fuck... I'm sorry, I'm sorry-
His father cuts him off, sighing.

They both take in the scattered papers surrounding Julie's desk. The drawers pulled out and picture frames faced flat. Beginning to put things back in order, Rowan slides off the chair and stands up. While shuffling through papers, Rowan notices an odd looking folder, stamped and closed. Opening it up, his dad suddenly stares at him, frozen.
Inside the folder lays a packet of legal sheets. Clipped to the front is a page titled 'Divorce Agreement'. Two signature lines sit at the very bottom, with only one name signed.
Of course I don't fucking understand! How could you do this?
We were going to tell you, but after everything happened... Listen, the decision was mutual.
Then where the fuck is her signature?
His father stays silent, looking away. Rowan stares him down.
And you're not? You missed your own mother's funeral, Rowan.
Rowan stumbles back, hurt by his dad's words. Dropping the papers, he turns around and rushes out of the room. His dad follows quickly after him.
He chases him down the stairs, calling after him.
Reaching the front door, Rowan swings it open and stops, not turning around.
I wish it had been you.
EXT. SETH'S HOUSE - FRONT YARD - NIGHT

Seth, a tall teenager stands on his porch wearing an 'RIP Liam Payne' t-shirt and a smirk on his face. He stubs out a cigarette, turning back toward the front door to peer inside his house. Inside is packed with teenagers laughing and dancing, a thin layer of smoke clings to the air. People scattered about the yard talk amongst themselves. A familiar car pulls up to the curb.
Recognizing the car, he calls out to his friend. He walks towards the edge of the yard. Rowan gets out of his car, meeting him in the middle.
I knew you'd cave! What's up?
Rowan quickly wipes the remaining tears from his eyes, looking away.
Seth is not discreet. People begin to stare at the two.
Yo, chill, chill. Come inside and I'll get you something.
The friends dap each other up. Seth puts an arm around Rowan's shoulders and steers him inside the house.

The pair squeeze their way through the crowd lingering around the kitchen island, where a mess of bottles and solo cups scatter along the surface. Seth grabs a cup and turns on the faucet, getting Rowan a drink of water.
My parents were getting a divorce.
A GIRL interrupts them from across the counter, sweaty and clearly intoxicated. Her bloodshot eyes focus on Rowan.
Rowan ignores Seth, focusing on the girl.
Thank you, yours is cool too.
The girl is wearing a stained HARVARD crop-top.
Seth smacks Rowan's shoulder, trying to get his attention.
Rowan turns back to Seth, who looks at the girl embarrassed and disgusted.
I heard you talking, funerals suck. Want some company?
The girl pulls out two differently colored pills from her pocket, offering them to Rowan.
What the fuck? Rowan, don't take that shit.
Both of them look at Rowan expectedly. Rowan peels the pills from her sweaty palm and swallows them dry. Seth pulls Rowan back, failing at his attempt to stop his friend.
Fuck off! You invited me here to have a good time, didn't you?
I invited you here because you're my fucking friend, Rowan.
Rowan scoffs and pushes away from the scene. Grabbing a bottle of alcohol, he follows the music into the living room.
INT. SETH'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
Multicolored lights flash across the room. Huge speakers blare music, drowning out Rowan's thoughts. Moving through the thick crowd of bodies, Rowan joins them.
Time passes quickly as the drugs take effect. Dancing to the music and downing his drink, Rowan's vision begins to distort his surroundings. The lights blend together, music fading in and out.
Rowan's depth perception warps, the careening people seeming much too close for comfort. As his breathing quickens, Rowan loses balance and falls to the ground, his drink crashing with him.

Peoples feet stomp across him, pinning him to the floor. Rowan calls out for help, but the music is too loud. He feels a pull at his neck, focusing on his chest to see that his necklace is missing. Crawling through the crowd, he searches for the cross, but can't see clear enough to find it.

His vision hyper-focusing, Rowan sees a glint ahead of him. Reaching as far as he can, he picks up the pieces of his broken necklace. Staring down at them, his face falls as his sense of reality kicks back in.

An overwhelming sorrow washes over him. Rowan hyperventilates as he pushes himself off the floor, clutching the pieces of the necklace in his fist. Stumbling to his feet, he rushes past the crowd towards the front door.
EXT. SETH'S HOUSE - FRONT YARD - NIGHT
Rushing out of the house, Rowan fumbles for his phone. Rowan dials a number, holding the phone up to his face. Fighting through his sobs, he attempts to catch a ride.
Hello?...I know it's late, I'm sorry... I'm okay. I need your help.
Rowan sits in the passengers seat as Marianne side-eyes him with a concerned look. His head is hung low, still breathing hard, staring at the broken parts of his necklace.
She was on the way to pick me up. I could've taken the bus like always, but I didn't want to. I didn't want to.
Rowan, slow down. It's not your fault.
My mom didn't need to drive that day. If it weren't for me, she'd still be here. My mom would still be here if it weren't for me, Marianne.
Rowan continues to spill out everything. He tells her about his absence at the funeral, the fight with his dad, the divorce, the party, and all the guilt he'd been carrying with him.
Soon enough, the two pull up to Rowan's driveway. His father is waiting for him on the front porch. A moment passes.
You are so strong, Rowan. It's time to face what's been happening. You can't keep running.
Rowan opens the car door, pushing the necklace into his pocket and stepping out.
EXT. ROWAN'S HOUSE - FRONT PORCH - NIGHT
Approaching his father, Rowan takes the crumbled paper back out of his pocket. Taking a deep breath, Rowan begins his speech.

Dear Mom, Do you remember my 7th birthday? I was so excited to open all the presents you and dad had kept hidden for weeks. The hallway closet isn't a good hiding spot, by the way. Dad rented an inflatable bouncy house for the backyard. You had made invitations for me to hand out at school the day before. An hour into the party I realized that none of my friends were going to show. You pushed me in the bouncy house to try to distract me from the empty yard. We didn't notice the thunder over the sound of our own laughter until the bouncy house roof sunk in, water pooling in the top. You shrieked out for dad and he ran in with an old mop, poking the caved in roof, but the rain just kept pouring. We gave up and Dad rushed out of the bouncy house, trying to shield us from the rain. When we made it inside soaking wet, you pulled out a small wrapped box from your back pocket. When I opened the box inside was a small silver cross necklace. I looked up at you with the biggest smile on my face and gave you the tightest hug, not wanting to let go. I didn't know how real that feeling was until now. Although its just a memory, I would do anything to come back to you. I love you, mom.
Rowan and his dad fall into each other.
